text: A nationwide campaign to transform school meals has gained momentum in recent months.
Nutritionists emphasize that children's diets have a profound impact on their cognitive development and long-term health.
Several cities have begun to integrate fresh, locally grown produce into cafeteria menus, replacing processed food with wholesome alternatives.
The initiative has not been without obstacles.
Budget constraints forced some districts to negotiate with local farmers for affordable supplies.
Critics dismiss the effort as merely symbolic, but early indicators suggest otherwise.
Schools that adopted the program report a notable decline in student absences and a surge in concentration levels.
Advocates insist that investing in children's nutrition is not a trivial matter — it is a fundamental obligation.
If the trend continues to thrive, it could reshape how an entire generation thinks about food.
